content.explanation: A REST API lets programs talk to a system (like a network controller or monitoring platform) over HTTP, the same protocol your browser uses. Instead of logging into a web page, your script sends an HTTP request to a URL and gets back structured data — usually in JSON format — that it can parse and act on. Different HTTP 'methods' mean different actions: reading data versus creating or changing it.

check.statement: Which HTTP method is conventionally used to retrieve/read data from an API without changing anything on the server?

solution.explanation: GET is the standard HTTP method for reading data without side effects. POST creates new data, PUT/PATCH update existing data, and DELETE removes it — 'SEND', 'FETCH', and 'PULL' aren't standard HTTP methods.
